main_ingredients: gochujang (sweet and spicy red pepper paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of Japanese short-grain rice topped with grilled beef yakiniku, fried garlic pieces, chili garlic flakes, seasoned bean sprouts and spinach, kimchi, gochujang paste, and a half-boiled egg, served with miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap (mixed rice with meat and vegetables) — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients and HPB Singapore Foodo analogous mixed rice bowl entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this specific Sukiya menu item exists in any approved primary database. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted component analysis: Japanese short-grain cooked rice (~45% by weight, USDA FDC #168878), grilled beef thin slices (~20%, USDA FDC #174032), seasoned vegetables including bean sprouts and spinach (~12%, USDA FDC analogues), kimchi (~8%, USDA FDC #169999), fried garlic (~3%, USDA FDC #169230), half-boiled egg (~8%, USDA FDC #173424), gochujang paste (~4%, USDA FDC #1100185). Values are cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo bibimbap category (approximately 130–160 kcal/100 g for mixed rice bowls with meat). Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) and mixed rice dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific yakiniku bibimbap bowl. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Japanese short-grain cooked rice (GI ~54–56 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2021) modulated downward slightly by the presence of protein (beef, egg), fat (fried garlic, beef fat), dietary fibre (vegetables, kimchi), and acidity (kimchi, gochujang). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 101.75) / 100 ≈ 55.0 g, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 18.5 × 5.5 = 101.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kimchi, miso soup, gochujang, and seasoned vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (≈2,310 mg per full serving), which substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 600–800 mg; requesting reduced-sodium condiments and omitting miso soup can partially mitigate intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by Japanese short-grain rice, but high glycemic load per serving, partially offset by protein, fat, and fermented vegetable components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in moderation given its low GI of approximately 54 but high glycemic load per serving (~55 g GL); pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, reducing rice portion, and monitoring post-prandial blood glucose is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and fried garlic, combined with high sodium from fermented condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of this dish's combined saturated fat (~1.6 g/100 g, ~8.8 g per serving) and high sodium load (~2,310 mg per serving), both of which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less gochujang and miso soup is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Yakiniku Bibimbap Bowl with Fried Garlic and Chili Garlic Flakes is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish served at Sukiya, a Japanese gyudon chain operating in Singapore. The bowl features thinly sliced beef cooked in yakiniku (Japanese-style grilled or pan-seared) seasoning, placed over a bed of 100% Japan-harvested short-grain rice. It is garnished with whole fried garlic pieces and chili garlic flakes for aromatic depth and heat. The bowl also contains seasoned bean sprouts and spinach (namul-style), kimchi (fermented spicy cabbage), and gochujang (a sweet and spicy Korean red pepper paste), creating a layered umami, tangy, and spicy flavour profile. A half-boiled egg is served on top, adding richness and creaminess. The combo is accompanied by miso soup. Texturally, the dish combines the chewiness of short-grain rice, the tenderness of grilled beef, the crunch of fried garlic, and the soft bite of seasoned vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~8.2 g/100 g) from beef and egg, moderate carbohydrates (~18.5 g/100 g) from rice, and moderate fat (~4.8 g/100 g). Sodium content is high (~420 mg/100 g) due to kimchi, miso, and gochujang, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low), modulated by protein, fat, and fermented components. This dish is relevant for clinical dietary counselling in the context of sodium restriction, glycaemic management in Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for this specific Sukiya menu item exists in any approved primary database (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis of individual ingredients using USDA FoodData Central data, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo analogous mixed rice bowl categories. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (Japanese short-grain cooked rice) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries and cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo bibimbap analogues; no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese chain restaurant bibimbap bowl portions; actual weight may vary. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component data for the individual ingredients used in this estimation; vitamin D values are not consistently available across the component ingredient entries (cooked rice, grilled beef, kimchi, egg, vegetables) in USDA FoodData Central, and no reliable aggregate value can be derived. 4. Sodium content is likely to vary significantly depending on the amount of kimchi, gochujang, and miso soup consumed. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers', which are not applicable to this dish and have been disregarded as erroneous field survey platform tags. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore has not been listed as a halal-certified restaurant by MUIS as of the knowledge cutoff. 7. GI estimated at 54 based on Japanese short-grain cooked rice GI from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021), modulated by protein, fat, and fermented components. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and should be treated as approximate.
